name: The Buddha
description: Teachings on the mind, suffering, and compassion from the Dhammapada.
author: Quips Editorial

quotes.content: All that we are is the result of what we have thought: it is founded on our thoughts, it is made up of our thoughts. If a man speaks or acts with an evil thought, pain follows him, as the wheel follows the foot of the ox that draws the carriage.
quotes.notes: The opening verse of the Twin-Verses chapter, teaching that action springs from mind; the popular paraphrase 'What you think you become' derives from this.

quotes.content: All that we are is the result of what we have thought: it is founded on our thoughts, it is made up of our thoughts. If a man speaks or acts with a pure thought, happiness follows him, like a shadow that never leaves him.
quotes.notes: The companion to verse 1, contrasting the reward of a pure mind with the pain of an evil one.

quotes.content: "He abused me, he beat me, he defeated me, he robbed me,"—in those who harbour such thoughts hatred will never cease.
quotes.notes: On how nursing grievances perpetuates hatred, from the Twin-Verses chapter.

quotes.content: "He abused me, he beat me, he defeated me, he robbed me,"—in those who do not harbour such thoughts hatred will cease.
quotes.notes: The counterpart to verse 3: releasing resentment ends hatred.

quotes.content: For hatred does not cease by hatred at any time: hatred ceases by love, this is an old rule.
quotes.notes: One of the most quoted lines of the Dhammapada, often rendered as 'hatred is never appeased by hatred.'

quotes.content: The world does not know that we must all come to an end here; but those who know it, their quarrels cease at once.
quotes.notes: On how awareness of mortality dissolves disputes.

quotes.content: As a fletcher makes straight his arrow, a wise man makes straight his trembling and unsteady thought, which is difficult to guard, difficult to hold back.
quotes.notes: From the chapter on the Mind, likening mental discipline to an arrow-maker's craft.

quotes.content: It is good to tame the mind, which is difficult to hold in and flighty, rushing wherever it listeth; a tamed mind brings happiness.
quotes.notes: From the chapter on the Mind, on the reward of self-mastery.

quotes.content: Whatever a hater may do to a hater, or an enemy to an enemy, a wrongly-directed mind will do us greater mischief.
quotes.notes: On how a misdirected mind harms more than any external enemy.

quotes.content: Not a mother, not a father will do so much, nor any other relatives; a well-directed mind will do us greater service.
quotes.notes: The counterpart to verse 42: a rightly-directed mind is the greatest benefactor.

quotes.content: Well-makers lead the water wherever they like; fletchers bend the arrow; carpenters bend a log of wood; wise people fashion themselves.
quotes.notes: On self-cultivation, from the chapter on the Wise; the same image recurs at verse 145.

quotes.content: The gods even envy him whose senses, like horses well broken in by the driver, have been subdued, who is free from pride, and free from appetites.
quotes.notes: From the chapter on the Venerable, praising mastery of the senses.

quotes.content: His thought is quiet, quiet are his word and deed, when he has obtained freedom by true knowledge, when he has thus become a quiet man.
quotes.notes: On the tranquility of one liberated by wisdom.

quotes.content: Even though a speech be a thousand of words, but made up of senseless words, one word of sense is better, which if a man hears, he becomes quiet.
quotes.notes: From the chapter on the Thousands, valuing meaning over quantity of words.

quotes.content: If one man conquer in battle a thousand times thousand men, and if another conquer himself, he is the greatest of conquerors.
quotes.notes: From the chapter on the Thousands, exalting self-conquest over military victory.

quotes.content: Let no man think lightly of evil, saying in his heart, It will not come nigh unto me. Even by the falling of water-drops a water-pot is filled; the fool becomes full of evil, even if he gather it little by little.
quotes.notes: The origin of the 'drops of water fill a pitcher' image, warning that small evils accumulate.

quotes.content: Let no man think lightly of good, saying in his heart, It will not come nigh unto me. Even by the falling of water-drops a water-pot is filled; the wise man becomes full of good, even if he gather it little by little.
quotes.notes: The counterpart to verse 121: small goods likewise accumulate into greatness.

quotes.content: Not in the sky, not in the midst of the sea, not if we enter into the clefts of the mountains, is there known a spot in the whole world where a man might be freed from an evil deed.
quotes.notes: From the chapter on Evil, on the inescapability of one's own deeds.

quotes.content: He who seeking his own happiness punishes or kills beings who also long for happiness, will not find happiness after death.
quotes.notes: From the chapter on Punishment, on non-harming and the wish of all beings for happiness.

quotes.content: By oneself the evil is done, by oneself one suffers; by oneself evil is left undone, by oneself one is purified. Purity and impurity belong to oneself, no one can purify another.
quotes.notes: From the chapter on Self, on personal responsibility for one's own purity.

quotes.content: Not to commit any sin, to do good, and to purify one's mind, that is the teaching of all the Awakened.
quotes.notes: A famous summary of the Buddha's teaching: avoid evil, do good, purify the mind.

quotes.content: Let us live happily then, not hating those who hate us! Among men who hate us let us dwell free from hatred!
quotes.notes: The opening of the chapter on Happiness, on dwelling without hatred.

quotes.content: Victory breeds hatred, for the conquered is unhappy. He who has given up both victory and defeat, he, the contented, is happy.
quotes.notes: From the chapter on Happiness, on the peace found beyond winning and losing.

quotes.content: Health is the greatest of gifts, contentedness the best riches; trust is the best of relationships, Nirvana the highest happiness.
quotes.notes: From the chapter on Happiness, ranking health, contentment, and trust among life's true goods.

quotes.content: Let a man overcome anger by love, let him overcome evil by good; let him overcome the greedy by liberality, the liar by truth.
quotes.notes: From the chapter on Anger, prescribing generous responses to hostility.

quotes.content: Let a wise man blow off the impurities of himself, as a smith blows off the impurities of silver, one by one, little by little, and from time to time.
quotes.notes: From the chapter on Impurity, comparing self-purification to a silversmith's refining.

quotes.content: The fault of others is easily perceived, but that of oneself is difficult to perceive; a man winnows his neighbour's faults like chaff, but his own fault he hides, as a cheat hides the bad die from the gambler.
quotes.notes: From the chapter on Impurity, on our readiness to see others' faults over our own.

quotes.content: You yourself must make an effort. The Awakened are only preachers. The thoughtful who enter the way are freed from the bondage of Mara.
quotes.notes: From the chapter on the Way; often paraphrased as 'Buddhas only point the way.' Muller renders 'the Awakened' for Tathagatas.

quotes.content: "All created things perish," he who knows and sees this becomes passive in pain; this is the way to purity.
quotes.notes: From the chapter on the Way, on impermanence as the path to release from suffering.

quotes.content: There is no suffering for him who has finished his journey, and abandoned grief, who has freed himself on all sides, and thrown off all fetters.
quotes.notes: From the chapter on the Venerable, describing the peace of one wholly liberated.
